name: NaN Archy
designer: Hélène Marian
classification_notes: Rooted in paint-brush lettering and processed through a digital lens, NaN Archy is a humanist sans with two distinct versions: one embracing structural glitches across a wide weight range, the other a restrained workhorse with subtle Art Deco proportions.
summary: A sans serif by Hélène Marian split into two versions: one that treats structural weight-range glitches as a deliberate feature, another that channels the same brush-lettering origins into a clean, Art Deco-tinged workhorse.
description: NaN Archy emerges from Marian's paint-brush lettering practice, then strips back most of the organic quality through digital treatment. The [ON] version preserves structural simplifications from the Ultra weight across the full range, so lighter weights reveal unexpected black spots that give text a rhythmic, score-like quality. The [OFF] version drops the glitches entirely, retaining humanist proportions and a faint Art Deco character suited to sustained reading and versatile use.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Archy as Hélène Marian's first release at the foundry, rooted in her paint-brush lettering practice but put through a highly digital treatment. The [ON] version explores how glyph structures shift across an extreme weight range, with the glitch treated as a feature rather than a flaw. The [OFF] version is more restrained, offering a humanist workhorse with a slight Art Deco fragrance. The family supports 311 Latin-based languages and was released in version 1.0 in June 2025, with all future updates included for Full Family licensees.

name: NaN Druid
designer: Anna Khorash & Reymund Schröder
classification_notes: The serif branch is Jenson-adjacent with a hybrid antique-to-slab quality and lower contrast suited to text; the paired sans shares the humanist structure with higher contrast and flared terminals betraying calligraphic origins. The family straddles display and text use across both subfamilies.
summary: A paired serif and sans-serif family by Anna Khorash and Reymund Schröder, rooted in Jenson-adjacent forms and inflected with esoteric ornamental detail across 286 Latin-based languages.
description: Designed collaboratively by Khorash and Schröder and released between 2022 and 2024, the family splits into two branches that share proportions and weights but diverge in contrast: the serif runs lower contrast for text friendliness while the sans runs higher contrast for display work. Both trace back to a humanist skeleton, with the sans showing flared and tapered terminals that give away its calligraphic roots. Glyphs throughout carry hidden figurative references drawn from nature and esoterica, from ampersands to paragraph marks.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Druid as a two-headed family conjured by Co-Archdruids Khorash and Schröder through a symbiotic, equal-authorship design process. Rooted in a Jenson-adjacent, hybrid antique-to-slab serif style, the sans borrows its sibling's humanist structure and applies it to open-countered letterforms with flared and tapered endings. Both families range from a monolinear hairline to a heavy black and support 286 Latin-based languages, with glyphs carrying hidden references to mistletoe, oak leaves, feathers and bones throughout.

name: NaN Fiasco
designer: Luke Prowse
classification_notes: A sans-serif rooted in modernist grotesque conventions but systematically subverted through deliberate errata: flipped glyphs, mis-weighting, naive construction and substitute forms. The underlying structure is grotesque; the treatment is wilfully aberrant.
summary: A grotesque sans by Luke Prowse that treats letterform error, misregistration and naive construction as a design system across nine weights from Thin to Ultra.
description: Prowse drew NaN Fiasco between 2016 and 2021, taking slipped vinyl, overlapping posters, flipped glyphs and miscalibrated weights as its structural logic rather than as faults to correct. The result sits against the cool neutrality of its modernist sources: where a rational grotesque is composed and consistent, Fiasco turns inconsistency into a rule, giving the family an irregular warmth. Nine weights from Thin to a notably broad Ultra make it usable across display and body contexts wherever some looseness is an asset.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Fiasco as a disobedient sans-serif that draws inspiration from errata in letterform design, application and reproduction, embracing glitches, slipped vinyl, overlapping posters, substitute forms, flipped glyphs, mis-weighting and naive construction. They frame it as a seemingly irrational treatment of a rational genre, where inconsistency-as-a-rule gives the typeface a mischievous warmth that separates it from its starker modernist sources.

name: NaN Holo
designer: Luke Prowse
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-Grotesk, combining rectangular counters with rounded curves on an industrial frame; the biological disruptions and proportional idiosyncrasies sit within the grotesque tradition rather than outside it.
summary: A neo-grotesque with a science-fiction premise, mixing rectangular counters and rounded curves across five width variants and a monospaced companion.
description: Designed by Luke Prowse with additional work from Jean-Baptiste Morizot and Fátima Lázaro, Holo pairs an industrial skeleton with deliberately inconsistent rounded and squared details that create warmth at display sizes and recede at text sizes. Wider-than-usual capitals give the rhythm a confident openness without tipping into novelty. The monospaced variant, Holo Mono, extends the family into code and technical contexts while maintaining the same underlying tension between machine logic and biological form.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Holo as a replicant-inspired, fleshy neo-Grotesk built on an industrial frame with biological forms disrupting an otherwise tensely machined flow, asking whether it passes a Voight-Kampff test. They note its dramatic versatility: at large sizes the tension between rounded and squared details brings surprise and character, while at small sizes those details blend into a sturdy workhorse. Holo Mono is positioned as the code at the typeface's foundation, humanity at the centre of circuitry.

name: NaN Hyena
designer: Reymund Schröder
classification_notes: A humanist sans extrapolated from the work of calligrapher and type designer Hildegard Korger, with three subfamilies that each interpret humanist letterform tradition through distinct terminal treatments ranging from concave to flat to rounded.
summary: A humanist sans in three subfamilies, each named for a time of day and distinguished by contrasting terminal treatments drawn from the calligraphic work of Hildegard Korger.
description: Designed by Reymund Schröder as a typographic extrapolation of 20th-century calligrapher Hildegard Korger's work, the family is structured around three named subfamilies: Sunrise with concave stem sections, Noon with flat terminals and settled neutrality, and Sunset with rounded forms and hair-thin connections. Rather than a conventional weight hierarchy, the three styles function as a spectrum of mood, intended to be mixed on the same page or even within the same word. Supporting 219 Latin-based languages, the family spans seven weights per subfamily.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Hyena as Reymund Schröder's crisp take on the humanist sans, framed within his practice of Typographic Uchronias or alternate type history. Extrapolated from Hildegard Korger's calligraphic work, the three subfamilies named Sunrise, Noon and Sunset recast typographic hierarchy as a spectrum of mood, invoking Monet's studies of Rouen Cathedral at different times of day as a conceptual parallel. Schröder encourages mixing the subfamilies together on the same page, word or even overlaid.

name: NaN Jaune
designer: Jérémy Landes
classification_notes: A closed-aperture grotesque sans with deliberately colliding glyph anatomy, structured across three subfamilies (Maxi, Midi, Mini) with varying optical characteristics from display to text use.
summary: A closed-aperture grotesque by Jérémy Landes that treats colliding glyph anatomy not as a flaw to avoid but as its central design premise, spread across three subfamilies from display Maxi to text-focused Mini.
description: Designed by Jérémy Landes between 2016 and 2022 with italic and Cyrillic by Vika Usmanova, Jaune inverts conventional type wisdom by deliberately pushing together anatomical elements that normally require clearance. Maxi is built for headlines with short extenders and extreme weight contrast from Thin to Black; Mini answers at the other end with open counters and deep ink traps for running text; Midi sits between the two as the general-purpose cut. The family covers 339 Latin-based and 16 Cyrillic-based languages.
foundry_blurb: NaN frame Jaune as the embodiment of their motto: take a bad idea and do it well, or at least die trying. Where convention steers away from colliding glyph anatomy, Jaune leans in, treating closed apertures and touching forms as a design opportunity rather than a problem. Three subfamilies span Maxi for compact display work, Midi as a confident all-rounder, and Mini for long-form reading with ink traps.

name: NaN Metrify
designer: Luke Prowse & J.B Morizot
classification_notes: NaN explicitly position Metrify within the Neo-Grotesque genre, drawing on Swiss International Style precedents while extending the form across nine widths and three distinct flavour variants. The family sits squarely in the neo-grotesque category but questions its constraints.
summary: A 108-style neo-grotesque available in three distinct flavour variants and six non-Latin scripts, spanning nine widths from Compressed to Gigawide.
description: Designed by Luke Prowse and J.B Morizot at NaN, Metrify answers the question of what a sans-serif should be in a genuinely transborder context, with dedicated Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Thai and Pan-African versions developed alongside script-specific consultants. The family is split into three ABC variants sharing a common skeleton: A is the most detailed and warm, B is stripped back and rational, and C pushes further toward a simplified near-geometric vocabulary. All styles are delivered as variable fonts, with nine widths running from Compressed to Gigawide across six weights and matching italics.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Metrify as their no-bullshit take on the Neo-Grotesque genre and their first world-type family. They questioned what internationalism actually means for type design, noting that Swiss International Style grotesques were international from the inside out, and set out to build a genuinely world-ready family by collaborating with consultants across Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Thai and Pan-African scripts. The three ABC flavours — each built on a shared skeleton — offer designers different entry points into the genre, from the industrially warm A, through the rational B, to the simplified near-geometric C.

name: NaN Rage
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: A sprawling type system spanning slab-serifs, early sans-serifs, a rounded variant, a script and an experimental cut, drawing on 19th-century wood-type and early computing aesthetics. No single category applies; display is the closest given the headline-first orientation and the sheer expressive range across seven distinct subfamilies.
summary: Seven matched font families spanning slabs, sans-serifs, a rounded cut, a script and an experimental variant, built as a single coherent type system with shared proportions and language support across 305 Latin-based languages.
description: Designed by the NaN team and released in 2024, the Rage Typographic Universe draws from 19th-century titling wood-types and early computer fonts to produce seven distinct but interchangeable families. Each ranges from Extra-Condensed to Standard in width and Thin to Black in weight, giving 36 styles per subfamily and 252 styles across the full system. The shared proportions and contrast mean any combination of Beau, Slab, Sans, Soft, Poly, Quik and Zipp can be mixed for editorial, branding or motion work without visual friction.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage as a Typographic Universe in and of itself: an ahistorical type system anchored in the present that pays homage to 19th-century titling wood-types and early computer fonts. The full collection of seven families covers two slabs, two sans-serifs, a rounded, a script and a weirdo, all with matching proportions, weights and language support for 305 Latin-based languages, available as a bundle for 450€.

name: NaN Serf
designer: Daria Cohen, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge
classification_notes: NaN describe it as a present-day old-style serif with orthogonal, perpendicular terminals and open counters — placing it squarely in the humanist old-style tradition but with a deliberate vectorial, geometric sharpness that modernises the genre.
summary: A contemporary old-style serif from NaN spanning four optical sizes and eight weights, with two stylistic variants distinguishing squared and rounded punctuation.
description: Designed across Display, Headline, Text and Micro optical sizes, the family uses perpendicular terminals and open counters to give old-style structure a sharp, vectorial quality that reads cleanly on screen and in print alike. Eight weights per optical size provide broad compositional range, and the addition of italics in version 1.1 completes a thorough typographic system. Two versions — Serf A with stylised squared punctuation and Serf B with traditional rounded forms — give designers a meaningful choice of graphic register within the same skeleton.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Serf as a present-day old-style serif built to perform at all sizes, with orthogonal detailing that brings a sharp, vectorial quality to a centuries-old genre. Perpendicular terminals, open counters and flat terminal endings snap cleanly to the pixel grid, making it web-ready for screen and print use across editorial, digital and branding contexts. The family covers 305 Latin-based languages, comes in TTF and WOFF2 formats, and is available in two versions: Serf A with stylised squared punctuation and Serf B with traditional rounded dots for the more conservative user.

name: NaN Serf Sans
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge
classification_notes: NaN describe it explicitly as a humanist sans derived from the NaN Serf skeleton, with vertically-cut stroke endings and generous counters optimised for text use on screen.
summary: A humanist sans derived from the NaN Serf skeleton, built for screen text across five scripts and more than 550 languages.
description: Sharing the underlying skeleton of NaN Serf, the design replaces serifs with clean vertical-cut endings and opens up the counters to prioritise rhythm and legibility at text sizes. Five scripts — Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Greek and Thai — are included from the outset, covering 482 Latin-based, 49 Cyrillic-based and 33 Arabic-based languages alongside Greek and Thai. Distinctive details such as the flat curves on the /G, /b and /q carry over from Serf, giving the family a consistent character across both subfamilies.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Serf Sans as their crisp, ultra-legible humanist sans built on the skeleton of NaN Serf, retaining distinctive details like the flat curves of the /G, /b and /q while replacing serifs with clean vertically-cut endings. Designed with modern screen needs in mind, it launched with five scripts covering more than 550 languages, with all future updates included free for Full Family licensees.

name: NaN Spaceland
designer: Jack Llewellyn
classification_notes: Based on a mono-linear grotesque skeleton but treated as display through its Boolean-overlap construction where outlines fold and intersect like a Möbius strip, making it conceptually and visually too distinctive for a straightforward sans-serif classification.
summary: A grotesque built on Boolean geometry, where glyph outlines overlap and fold like a Möbius strip to simultaneously read as positive and negative form.
description: Designed by Jack Llewellyn as his first release with NaN, the typeface takes a minimal grotesque structure and subjects it to a Boolean logic: outlines intersect and cross over one another, creating collision points that confuse inner and outer space. In lighter weights the effect is subtle, lending a quiet elegance; in heavier weights the breaks become emphatic, tipping the letterforms towards the illustrative. The family covers 310 Latin-based languages and was developed across 2022 to 2024 with additional production by Léon Hugues and Jean Baptiste Morizot.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Spaceland as Jack Llewellyn's first typeface with the foundry, conceived around two interests: designs that are inherently digital in nature, and the question of whether component shapes can be positive and negative simultaneously. Drawing on the Boolean principle of intersection, the glyphs feature overlapping outlines that read like a Möbius strip, collapsing distinctions between white space and black space. The family is named after characters from E. A. Abbott's Flatland: A Romance of Many Dimensions.

name: NaN Success
designer: Jérémy Landes
classification_notes: A three-subfamily display system drawing from late Art Nouveau sources such as De Vinne and Louis Jou, with an ornate titling sub-family packed with ligatures and alternates. The Display classification is appropriate given the titling-first orientation of the family, though Success Text extends usability to body sizes.
summary: A three-part display family by Jérémy Landes rooted in late Art Nouveau lettering, carrying 280 ligatures in the Titling sub-family and extending through a condensed gothic Sans and a text-weight workhorse, all covering Latin and Cyrillic.
description: The family splits into three distinct but related sub-families: Success Titling, which leans heavily into ornamental ligatures including multi-width variants that mix compressed and wide glyphs for a hand-lettering quality; Success Sans, which takes the same organic curves onto a condensed gothic structure ranging from lace-like Light to a steampunk-inflected Black; and Success Text, a tuned-down version of the titling DNA suited to long reading at small sizes. Latin coverage spans 317 languages and Cyrillic 44. NaN donate 15% of revenue from the family to ClientEarth.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Success as an essay on titling typography, drawing on late Art Nouveau references including De Vinne and Louis Jou. The Titling sub-family carries 280 ligatures for Latin and Cyrillic, including multi-width ligatures that blend compressed and wide glyphs to evoke hand-lettering. Success Sans takes the family's curves onto a condensed gothic skeleton, while Success Text offers a warm, low-contrast text companion. The family was designed by Jérémy Landes with additional production by Fátima Lázaro, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, and released across 2021 and 2022.

name: NaN SuperX Serif
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: High-contrast old-style structure with leaf-shaped teardrops and a calligraphic italic rooted in pointed-nib forms; the disproportionately large x-height and phototypesetting references place it within a contemporary revival of old-style proportions rather than a straight historical model.
summary: A high-contrast old-style serif with an oversized x-height, leaf-shaped teardrops and a pointed-nib italic, spanning Latin and Arabic across three optical sizes.
description: Designed by the NaN team and released across 2024 and 2025, it takes the disproportionately large x-height of phototypesetting-era faces as its starting point. Eight weights with italics run across Display, Headline and Text optical sizes, with the Text cut reducing contrast and loosening spacing for smaller settings. Details are carefully calibrated: vivid round shapes sit alongside angular tails on the /a, sharp apexes on /V and /W, and vertical serifs on /C and /Z, giving the face an identifiable voice without tipping into novelty.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe SuperX Serif as a homage to phototypesetting-era typefaces characterised by a disproportionately large x-height. It forms one half of the SuperX system alongside the Sans, with the two families designed to pair together for campaign headlines at tight line-heights. The Serif features leaf-shaped teardrops and a dynamic italic rooted in pointed-nib calligraphy, and the addition of optical sizes in version 1.1 extends the system's usability into text settings. The family supports 482 Latin-based languages and 33 Arabic-based languages.

name: NaN SuperX Sans
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Described by NaN as a hybrid humanist sans with grotesque detailing: open counters and vertical terminals from a humanist structure, combined with closed neo-grotesque forms on round letters such as /c /e /g with horizontal terminals.
summary: A hybrid humanist sans with grotesque detailing, drawn with a disproportionately large x-height as a tribute to phototypesetting-era typefaces, available in eight weights with italics across three optical sizes in both Latin and Arabic.
description: The sans half of NaN's SuperX system, it borrows its proportions and open counters from SuperX Serif while adopting closed neo-grotesque horizontal terminals on round letters, producing an unusual tension between the two traditions. Three optical sizes — Display, Headline and Text — allow the family to move from tight campaign headlines down to sustained body copy without losing its character. Arabic support runs alongside Latin across 482 and 33 language variants respectively, making it one of the more thoroughly engineered releases in NaN's catalogue.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe SuperX Sans as a hybrid: a humanist sans with grotesque detailing, or the contrary. Its structure and proportions derive from SuperX Serif, giving it open counters and vertical cuts in terminals, while round letters adopt a closed neo-grotesque form with horizontal terminals for what NaN call a vivid and surprising — quite jolly — character. The family spans 8 weights plus italics in three optical sizes, with the Text optical size reducing x-height and contrast slightly for bulletproof legibility. Designed between 2024 and 2025 with additional engineering by Igino Marini.

name: NaN Tragedy
designer: Jean-Baptiste Morizot
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif with a pronounced calligraphic stress axis and classical skeleton, sitting between transitional and contemporary serif traditions. The exaggerated contrast and unconventional details resist easy placement within historical categories.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Jean-Baptiste Morizot that pairs a classical calligraphic skeleton with exaggerated geometrical detail, spanning Text, Display and Mono cuts.
description: Built around an exaggerated diagonal stress axis and unconventional letterforms set onto a classical skeleton, the family runs across three distinct subfamilies — Text, Display and Mono — developed between 2019 and 2023. The Display cut pushes contrast and drama further, while the Text cut applies optical corrections to keep the idiosyncratic forms functional at smaller sizes. Tragedy Mono is the most singular member, forcing the modulated diagonal stress into a monospaced grid to produce a texture rarely encountered in that format.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Tragedy as a chimera of two epochs, its structure rooted in the pace of a religious scribe while its geometrical detail treatment belongs to the present. They frame it as a study in oppositions — classical yet contemporary, extravagant yet simple — with the Display cut embracing theatrical excess and the Text cut applying an economical approach with optical corrections. Tragedy Mono pushes the family's contradictions furthest, applying technological monospace constraints to a design inspired by an earlier era.

name: NaN Tresor
designer: Christoph Koeberlin
classification_notes: An anachronous revival drawing on historical serif structures, with multiple glyph variations per character across optical sizes; its bracketed serifs, ink-trap joins and classical proportions place it firmly in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: A historical serif revival by Christoph Koeberlin that deliberately preserves contradictory glyph structures across three optical sizes rather than rationalising them into a single system.
description: Rather than selecting one authoritative form for each character, Koeberlin collected as many historical and fictional variants as possible, giving each style up to eight alternates per glyph. Three optical cuts run from S, spaced for text and long-form reading with thin joins, through M as a workhorse across all scales, to L which ships with the most exuberant alternates by default for titling and display. The Complete Family adds Tresor Swash, an alternate version of the L cut with ornamental caps and swashes active from the outset.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Tresor as an anachronous revival that sidesteps the usual rationalisation of historical inconsistencies, instead offering the widest possible range of glyph variations within a single typeface. Designed by Christoph Koeberlin and developed between 2018 and 2022, it supports 420 Latin-based languages and is available in three optical sizes, S through L, with a Swash variant included in the Complete Family licence.

name: NaN Weiss
designer: Luke Prowse
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface derived from Emil Rudolf Weiß' calligraphic Weiss-Kapitale Kräftig; the monospace constraint places it firmly in the mono category, while its scriptural, humanist origins distinguish it from geometric or technical monospaces.
summary: A monospace revival of Emil Rudolf Weiß' Weiss-Kapitale Kräftig by Luke Prowse, forcing a humanist calligraphic hand into the cold regularity of a fixed-width grid.
description: Designed by Luke Prowse between 2019 and 2020, NaN Weiss transplants the scriptural warmth of Weiß' early twentieth-century lettering into the metronomic constraints of a monospace. The collision produces something genuinely strange: letterforms that carry religious and humanist associations yet sit locked to a mechanical grid, described by NaN as an alien almost successfully pretending to be human. Ten weights from Thin to Extra Black give the family unusual expressive range for a fixed-width typeface, covering 219 Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe NaN Weiss as a reimagining and extension of Emil Rudolf Weiß' Weiss-Kapitale Kräftig filtered through a fixed-width frame. They frame the result as a clash of times and worlds — the humanist against the algorithmic, the religious against the technocratic — jarring but warm, as if a foreign intelligence were almost convincingly passing as human. The family spans ten weights from Thin to Extra Black and supports 219 Latin-based languages, available in TTF and WOFF2.

name: NaN Holo Gigawide Ultra
designer: Luke Prowse
classification_notes: NaN describe Holo as a neo-Grotesk built on an industrial frame with biological forms; rectangular counters combined with round curves place it squarely in the neo-grotesque category with display-oriented proportions.
summary: A dramatically wide, single-style cut from NaN's broader Holo family, offered as a free download under a CC BY-ND licence.
description: NaN Holo Gigawide Ultra is the widest and heaviest member of the 36-style Holo family, designed by Luke Prowse with additional work from the NaN team. The typeface blends rectangular counters with rounded curves in a way that reads as simultaneously archaic and futuristic, drawing explicit conceptual reference to Blade Runner's Voight-Kampff test. At large sizes the tension between its squared and rounded details is felt as warmth and surprise; at smaller sizes those details collapse into a workable, personality-laden text face.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Holo as a replicant-inspired, fleshy neo-Grotesk built on an industrial frame disrupted by biological forms, available in proportional and monospaced styles. The Gigawide Ultra cut is positioned as the biggest and broadest of the entire family, and is offered as a free download under a Creative Commons BY-ND licence to anyone who joins their mailing list.

name: Unbounded
designer: Luke Prowse, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Fátima Lazaro, Florian Runge
classification_notes: A display sans with a hybrid construction mixing grotesque letterforms with humanist details, further distinguished by chevron-shaped stems and pinched counters, placing it between geometric and humanist sans traditions.
summary: An open-source display sans with a hybrid grotesque-humanist construction, funded by the Polkadot treasury and covering over 400 Latin and Cyrillic-based languages.
description: Six display weights from Extra Light to Black, plus a variable font, built around a mix of grotesque and humanist letterforms that coexist with unusual coherence. Chevron-shaped stems on key glyphs such as /B, /E, /a and /d break the regularity of straight stems, while pinched, fish-like counters on bowl letters give the face a distinctly friendly character. Rare attention to under-supported African languages, including the Pan-Nigerian alphabet, extends its utility well beyond the typical Latin and Cyrillic offering.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Unbounded as possibly the first open source, freely available and on-chain funded font in the world, made possible by the Polkadot treasury. The typeface blends grotesque and humanist construction in what they call an original but surprisingly cohesive harmony, with chevron-shaped stems and pinched counters setting it apart. Over 1,300 glyphs cover more than 400 languages across Latin and Cyrillic scripts, with particular care given to under-supported African languages such as those using the Pan-Nigerian alphabet.

name: NaN Rage Beau
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describe it as Clarendon-esque with thick rectangular serifs, ball terminals and bracket-less serifs, placing it squarely in the Victorian slab-serif tradition updated with contemporary geometry.
summary: A contemporary Clarendon-esque slab-serif spanning nine weights and four widths, balancing organic stroke contrast with bracket-less geometric serifs and ball terminals.
description: Part of NaN's Rage Typographic Universe, Rage Beau draws on Victorian slab-serif heritage without leaning into period idiosyncrasies. Ball terminals and thick rectangular serifs give it confident structure, while the stroke contrast and generously curved forms soften the geometry enough for sustained text setting. Four widths from Standard to XCondensed and nine weights across each make it adaptable from narrow headline columns to comfortable body copy.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Beau as a Clarendon-esque slab-serif with a contemporary personality, balancing organic qualities such as stroke contrast and generous curves against geometric details like bracket-less serifs and circular rounds. It is positioned as both a strong titling typeface and a capable running-text workhorse, available in four widths and nine weights to allow precise fitting across a range of typographic situations. Beau is part of the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe and supports 305 Latin-based languages.

name: NaN Rage Slab
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Bracket-less serifs, horizontal stroke endings borrowed from neo-grotesques, and simplified non-decorative forms place this firmly in the geometric/neo-grotesque branch of the slab-serif genre rather than the traditional Clarendon or Egyptian lineages.
summary: A bracket-less slab-serif spanning four widths and nine weights, built on neo-grotesque principles with sharp, simplified forms and perfectly horizontal stroke endings.
description: Part of the NaN Rage Typographic Universe, Rage Slab strips the slab-serif of its historical ornament, replacing bracketed serifs and decorative curves with clean interruptions and horizontal terminals drawn from neo-grotesque convention. The result reads cooler and more restrained than its sibling Rage Beau, running from Thin to Black across Standard, Narrow and XCondensed widths for use from dense body text columns to broad display headlines. Supporting 305 Latin-based languages, it is a thoroughly ahistorical take on the genre with a sharp, contemporary voice.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Slab as a modern take on the slab-serif genre, applying a less-is-more approach to produce bracket-less, simplified, non-decorative forms with sharp interruptions and perfectly horizontal stroke endings borrowed from neo-grotesques. Available in four widths and nine weights, it is positioned as a cooler, sleeker sibling to Rage Beau and a contemporary herald of the slab-serif genre within the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Rage Sans
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge
classification_notes: Described as a subtly contrasted sans with 1800s undertones and humanist warmth; the gentle contrast, open counters and delicate endings place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition rather than a cold geometric or grotesque one.
summary: A subtly contrasted humanist sans with 19th-century warmth, offered across four widths and nine weights for precise typographic control from narrow headlines to running text.
description: Part of NaN's Rage Typographic Universe alongside Rage Beau and Rage Slab, this family balances cold detailing — horizontal stroke endings, square punctuation — against genuinely warm proportions and open counters. Four widths from Standard to XCondensed give it unusual range, spanning editorial text columns through to tightly set display work. Supporting 305 Latin-based languages makes it a practical choice for multilingual publishing at any scale.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Sans as a subtly contrasted sans inheriting gentle 1800s undertones from its Rage Beau and Slab siblings. They highlight its mix of sleek cold details with warmth and delicacy, noting that thinner stroke endings open up the counters for a bright, legible character. The family spans 4 widths and 9 weights, supporting 305 Latin-based languages.

name: NaN Rage Soft
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: A subtly contrasted monolinear sans with fully hemispheric stem endings, placing it firmly in the rounded sans category. The page explicitly positions it between Caslon's 1836 rounded sans and Arial Rounded.
summary: A rounded sans with hemispheric stem endings and four widths, designed as the softest member of NaN's Rage Typographic Universe.
description: Part of a wider type system, Rage Soft extends the Rage family into rounded territory by applying fully hemispheric terminal cuts to a subtly contrasted monolinear structure. Nine weights across Standard, Narrow and XCondensed widths give it genuine versatility from tightly-set display headlines down to modest text sizes. The rounded forms avoid the goofy register of many rounded sans faces by retaining some of the curve tension found in its sibling cuts.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Soft as the softest member of the Rage Typographic Universe, sitting between the first rounded sans drawn by Caslon in 1836 and a more contemporary Arial Rounded. With fully hemispheric stem endings and four widths at nine weights each, it is designed for both narrow display titles and fitted columns of text, and is available as part of a family deal covering the entire Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Rage Poly
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: A curveless sans-serif where every curve has been replaced by straight polygonal segments, giving it a geometric and angular quality that sits between constructivist geometry and digital glitch aesthetics.
summary: A curveless sans-serif across four widths and nine weights, each curve replaced by straight polygonal segments to produce a strikingly angular texture that shifts from subtle at heavy weights to visibly glitchy at lighter ones.
description: Part of the NaN Rage Typographic Universe, Poly strips every curve from a sans-serif skeleton and replaces them with chains of straight segments, an approach with roots in geometry, cubism and futurism without being reducible to any one of them. The effect is most dramatic in lighter weights, where the broken arcs produce a glitchy, almost digital noise, while heavier weights at small sizes read closer to a conventional grotesque. Details such as flat diamond periods and tittles reinforce the polygonal logic throughout, and the four-width range from Standard to XCondensed gives the family genuine versatility across headline and text contexts.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Poly as a curveless sans-serif where every curve has been replaced by straight segments, an effect hard to pin to geometry, technology, cubism or futurism alone. The glitchy quality is most visible in lighter weights, while heavier weights at small sizes appear almost conventional. Thoughtful details like flat diamond periods and tittles reinforce the polygonal character throughout. Available in four widths and nine weights, and offered as part of the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Rage Quik
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Rooted in Sharpie pen lettering and graffiti, Rage Quik is a script typeface with broken angular forms, closed counters and a minimal slant angle, placing it firmly in the informal/expressive script category rather than calligraphic or formal script traditions.
summary: A graffiti-rooted script with closed counters and angular forms, available across four widths and nine weights for display and text setting.
description: Drawing from Sharpie pen lettering and graffiti culture, Rage Quik maintains flow while introducing broken, angular structures and a deliberately shallow slant. Closed counters on key glyphs such as /a and /e replace conventional bowls with single straight strokes, giving it a distinct tightness unusual for script typefaces. The 36-style range spanning four widths from Standard to XCondensed and nine weights from Thin to Black makes it unusually versatile for both narrow display headlines and longer text columns.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Quik as an unapologetic script font rooted in Sharpie pen lettering and graffiti, free of any particular school and characterised by irregularity. It is dynamic in its broken, angular structures yet maintains flow with a very small slant angle, and features closed counters on key glyphs that replace bowls with a single straight stroke. Available in four widths and nine weights, it forms part of the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe, designed by the NaN team with additional engineering by Igino Marini and released in 2024 with support for 305 Latin-based languages.

name: NaN Rage Zipp
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Derived from NaN Rage Poly, Zipp strips curves and points to geometric extremes, producing diamond-shaped rounds and spike-like stems that place it firmly in the experimental display category, drawing on glitch aesthetics and German expressionist woodcut traditions.
summary: A deliberately destabilised display type that replaces curves with diamond forms and sharp spikes, drawing equally from glitch culture and German expressionist woodcuts.
description: Derived from NaN Rage Poly, Zipp takes the reduction of bezier curves and points to its logical extreme: round letters become diamonds balanced on their corners and stems taper to needle-sharp points. Across four widths and nine weights, the family spans from compressed headline setting to usable text columns where individual letters sit like small sculptures on the page. It belongs to NaN's wider Rage Typographic Universe, sharing structural DNA with its siblings while occupying the furthest, most fractured position in the series.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Zipp as an experiment gone wrong, derived from Rage Poly by pushing the deletion of curves and points to its limit. The result, they say, is a graphical typeface that is a child of the glitch and of German expressionist woodcuts, where round letters become diamonds and stems become spiky toothpicks. Available in four widths and nine weights each, it is part of the broader NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Archy
designer: Hélène Marian
classification_notes: Rooted in paint-brush lettering and processed through digital abstraction, the typeface retains humanist proportions and structure across both its ON and OFF versions, with the OFF version particularly showing Art Deco-inflected humanist sans characteristics.
summary: A paint-brush-derived humanist sans in two versions: one embracing structural glitches across a wide weight range, the other a restrained workhorse with a faint Art Deco character.
description: Designed by Hélène Marian and released in 2024–2025, NaN Archy splits into two subfamilies. The ON version deliberately preserves the structural simplifications introduced at Ultra weight across lighter cuts, so that what would normally be invisible construction decisions become visible rhythm at small sizes. The OFF version drops the glitches but keeps the humanist proportions and subtle brush influence, making it viable for sustained text setting with a quiet decorative undertone.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Archy as Hélène Marian's first release at the foundry, taking its roots in her paint-brush lettering practice but processed through a highly digital treatment that suppresses most of its organic nature. The ON version explores how glyph structures are altered across a huge weight range from thin to ultra, embracing contradiction so that glitch becomes a feature. The OFF version is more restrained, abandoning glitches while keeping humanist structure and proportions, with a slight Art Deco fragrance drawn from the paint-brush influence.

name: NaN Druid
designer: Anna Khorash & Reymund Schröder
classification_notes: The serif member of the family is described as Jenson-adjacent with hybrid antique and slab serif characteristics and lower contrast suited to text setting; the sans counterpart shares humanist structure with flared and tapered endings that betray calligraphic origins.
summary: A paired serif and sans family by Anna Khorash and Reymund Schröder, rooted in Jenson-adjacent humanist forms and built for both text and display use across 286 Latin-based languages.
description: The serif draws on antique and slab serif traditions with deliberately lower contrast to keep it comfortable at text sizes, while the sans applies the same humanist skeleton with higher contrast and open counters, its flared and tapered terminals giving away its calligraphic origins. Both families run from Hairline to Black and share matching proportions, making them genuinely interchangeable across a single layout. Glyphs such as the ampersands and paragraph marks carry decorative details drawn from nature and esoteric imagery, giving the family an unusual specificity of character.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Druid as a two-headed family conjured by Co-Archdruids Reymund Schröder and Anna Khorash through a symbiotic, equal-authorship design process. The serif is Jenson-adjacent, oscillating between antique and slab traditions, while Druid Sans borrows its sibling's humanist structure and applies it to a high-contrast display-capable sans with open counters and calligraphic endings. Both families cover 286 Latin-based languages and range from a delicate monolinear Hairline to Black, with decorative glyphs incorporating natural and esoteric references including mistletoe, oak leaves, feathers and bones.

name: NaN Serf Sans
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge
classification_notes: NaN describe it explicitly as a humanist sans derived from the skeleton of NaN Serf, with vertically-cut stroke endings, open apertures and generous counters characteristic of the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A screen-first humanist sans drawn from the skeleton of NaN Serf, retaining its flat curves while replacing serifs with vertically-cut endings across five scripts and over 550 languages.
description: Built on the same underlying structure as NaN Serf, it trades that family's serifs for clean vertical terminals, preserving idiosyncratic details such as the flat curves on the /G, /b and /q. Generous counters and wide apertures give it strong legibility at text sizes on screen, while a carefully controlled rhythm makes it equally comfortable in extended reading. With out-of-the-box support for Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Greek and Thai, it is one of the more ambitious multilingual text releases in recent memory.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Serf Sans as their crisp and ultra-legible humanist sans, grown from the skeleton of NaN Serf with serifs replaced by clean vertically-cut endings. Designed with modern screen use in mind, the team paid particular attention to rhythm and counter space, and shipped the family with five script systems covering more than 550 languages from day one. Version 1.0 was released in February 2025, with all future updates included for Full Family licensees.

name: NaN SuperX Serif
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: High-contrast old-style serif with leaf-shaped teardrops and a calligraphic italic rooted in pointed-nib tradition, drawing on phototypesetting-era proportions with a disproportionately large x-height.
summary: A high-contrast old-style serif with phototypesetting-era proportions, leaf-shaped teardrops and a pointed-nib italic, available in eight weights across three optical sizes in both Latin and Arabic.
description: Designed by the NaN team and released in 2024, NaN SuperX Serif is built around an exaggerated x-height referencing the phototypesetting era, with detailing that balances vivid round forms against sleek angular details: vertical serifs on /C/S/Z, sharp apexes on /V/W and a dynamic italic drawn from pointed-nib calligraphy. Three optical sizes — Text, Headline and Display — allow the family to move from body copy to campaign headlines without losing its character. Covering 482 Latin-based and 33 Arabic-based languages, it pairs directly with NaN SuperX Sans, which shares the same underlying structure and proportions.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe SuperX Serif as their homage to phototypesetting-era typefaces characterised by a disproportionately large x-height. They frame it as part of the SuperX system — a serif and sans pairing — noting that the italic is rooted in pointed-nib calligraphy and that three optical sizes bring the family's character down to text sizes without sacrificing legibility. Every licensee of the Full Family package receives future updates at no additional cost.

name: NaN SuperX Sans
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: NaN describe it as a hybrid humanist sans with grotesque detailing, borrowing its main structure from the companion serif while closed neo-grotesque counters on /c /e /g introduce a contrasting tension between open humanist and closed grotesque forms.
summary: A hybrid humanist sans with grotesque detailing, built on a disproportionately large x-height drawn in homage to phototypesetting-era typefaces and released across three optical sizes in 8 weights with Latin and Arabic support.
description: Its structure and proportions are shared with the companion NaN SuperX Serif, producing open counters and vertical terminal cuts on /a /s /S, while /c /e /g take on a closed neo-grotesque form with horizontal terminals. Three optical sizes — Text, Headline and Display — adjust x-height, contrast, aperture and spacing to suit setting sizes from body copy to billboard. The family covers 482 Latin-based and 33 Arabic-based languages across all weights and italics.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe SuperX Sans as a hybrid: a humanist sans with grotesque detailing, or the contrary, drawing its main structure from the SuperX Serif to produce distinct open counters and vertical terminal cuts, while round letters embrace a closed neo-grotesque structure for a vivid, surprising character they call 'quite jolly'. Version 1.1, released October 2025, added optical sizes across every weight, with all Full Family licensees receiving updates at no extra cost.

name: NaN Holo Gigawide Ultra
designer: Luke Prowse
classification_notes: NaN describe Holo as a neo-Grotesk with biological forms disrupting an industrial frame; rectangular counters paired with rounded curves place it firmly in the neo-grotesque category with an unusually expressive, hybrid character.
summary: The widest and heaviest cut of NaN's Holo family, a replicant-inspired neo-grotesque with rectangular counters and rounded curves built for large-scale display use.
description: NaN Holo GigaWide Ultra sits at the extreme end of a 36-style family originally designed by Luke Prowse, with additional design from the NaN studio. Its defining tension is between squared counters and organic curves, a combination that gives it an atemporal quality, legible and familiar at reading sizes yet visually charged when set large. Available free under a CC BY-ND licence, it covers 219 Latin-based languages and comes with alternate OpenType forms for several characters.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Holo as a replicant-inspired, fleshy neo-Grotesk built on an industrial frame with biological forms disrupting the otherwise machined flow. The GigaWide Ultra is the broadest and heaviest style in the family, made available as a free download under a CC BY-ND licence. The wider-than-usual capitals are cited as a deliberate design decision, giving text rhythm a confident, comfortable breath. Holo Mono is also part of the family, positioned as the code at the typeface's foundation.

name: NaN Rage Beau
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Explicitly described as Clarendon-esque with thick rectangular bracket-less serifs and ball terminals, placing it firmly in the slab serif category with a Clarendon lineage distinguished by its mix of organic stroke contrast and geometric detailing.
summary: A contemporary Clarendon-lineage slab serif spanning 4 widths and 9 weights, balancing organic stroke contrast with bracket-less geometric serifs and ball terminals.
description: Part of NaN's broader Rage Typographic Universe, Rage Beau draws on Victorian slab serif heritage without period affectation, combining generous curves and stroke contrast with plain, bracket-less serifs and near-circular ball terminals. Four widths from Standard to XCondensed give the family genuine range, covering display settings at extreme sizes down to body text columns. The result sits credibly between a strong titling face and a workable running-text type across 305 Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Beau as a Clarendon-esque slab serif with a sleek and contemporary personality, designed to balance organic qualities such as stroke contrast and generous curves against geometric details including bracket-less serifs and circular rounds. Available in 4 widths and 9 weights, it is positioned as both a titling face and a running-text workhorse, and forms part of the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Rage Slab
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Bracket-less serifs, perfectly horizontal terminals and simplified non-decorative forms explicitly borrow from neo-grotesque conventions, placing this firmly in a contemporary, ahistorical strand of the slab-serif genre.
summary: A bracket-less slab-serif built across four widths and nine weights, drawing its clean horizontal terminals from the neo-grotesque tradition.
description: Part of the NaN Rage Typographic Universe, Rage Slab strips the slab-serif of ornamental habit: no brackets, no decorative stress, just sharp interruptions and flat endings that read more neo-grotesque than Victorian. The four widths, from Standard through to XCondensed, give the family genuine range from text columns at small sizes to towering condensed display headlines. Compared to its sibling Rage Beau, it runs cooler and more restrained, prioritising utility over warmth.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Slab as a modern take on the slab-serif genre, applying a "less is more" approach that results in bracket-less serifs, simplified non-decorative forms and sharp interruptions. They note its perfectly horizontal endings borrow from the neo-grotesque, and position it as a cooler, sleeker counterpart to Rage Beau within the broader NaN Rage Typographic Universe. Four widths and nine weights per width allow precise fine-tuning for both narrow titling and body text.

name: NaN Rage Sans
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge
classification_notes: A subtly contrasted sans with 19th-century undertones, warm detailing and open counters that place it in the humanist sans tradition, though cold geometric touches like horizontal stroke endings add a distinctive hybrid character.
summary: A subtly contrasted sans in four widths and nine weights, combining warm 1800s inflections with cold geometric detailing across 305 Latin-based languages.
description: Part of NaN's Rage Typographic Universe alongside Rage Beau and Rage Slab, this family blends delicate stroke contrast and open counters with sharp horizontal terminals and square punctuation. The four widths, Standard, Narrow and XCondensed, each running nine weights from Thin to Black, give it genuine range from dense editorial columns to broad display settings. The hybrid character, part warm humanist, part cool grotesque, makes it unusually versatile without feeling undecided.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Sans as a subtly contrasted sans inheriting gentle 1800s undertones from its Rage Beau and Slab cousins, mixing cold sleek detailing such as perfectly horizontal endings and square punctuation with a sense of delicacy and warmth. Its thinner endings allow for relatively open counters, giving it a bright and pleasant character with increased legibility. Available in four widths and nine weights, it is part of the broader NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Rage Poly
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: A curveless sans-serif where all curves are replaced with straight polygonal segments, giving it a geometric and angular character distinct from conventional grotesque or humanist sans families.
summary: A sans-serif in which every curve is replaced with straight polygonal segments, producing a glitchy, angular quality that shifts dramatically across its nine weights and four widths.
description: Part of the NaN Rage Typographic Universe, Poly pushes a single formal constraint — no curves whatsoever — across 36 styles spanning four widths from Standard to XCondensed and nine weights from Thin to Black. The effect is subtle in heavier weights at small sizes but becomes conspicuous and expressive in the lighter cuts, where the faceted outlines read as both geometric and slightly corrupted. Small details such as flat diamond periods and tittles reinforce the polygonal logic rather than treating it as mere surface novelty.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Poly as a curveless sans-serif in which every curve has been replaced by a series of straight segments, an effect they trace to geometry, technology, cubism and futurism without committing to any single origin. Available in four widths and nine weights, it supports 305 Latin-based languages and forms part of the broader NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Rage Quik
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Rooted in Sharpie pen lettering and graffiti, NaN Rage Quik is an informal script with broken angular structures, a minimal slant, and idiosyncratic closed counters that separate it from conventional calligraphic or brush script traditions.
summary: A graffiti- and Sharpie-inflected script with 36 styles across four widths and nine weights, part of NaN's broader Rage Typographic Universe.
description: Drawing on Sharpie pen lettering and graffiti rather than any formal calligraphic tradition, Rage Quik combines angular, broken structures with a low slant angle that keeps text readable at smaller sizes. Closed counters on key glyphs such as /a and /e replace conventional bowls with single straight strokes, giving the face a distinctly abrupt character. The four widths from Standard to XCondensed allow it to move between tight headline columns and display settings without losing its edge.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Quik as an unapologetic script with roots in Sharpie pen lettering and graffiti, calling it a free spirit not beholden to any particular school. Available in 4 widths and 9 weights, it supports 305 Latin-based languages and is part of the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe, engineered with additional input from Igino Marini.

name: NaN Rage Zipp
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: Derived from NaN Rage Poly by stripping curves and points to produce angular, glitch-like letterforms; the result is too distorted for body text use and functions primarily as a display or expressive typeface.
summary: A radical reduction of NaN Rage Poly in which all curves have been stripped out, leaving diamond-shaped rounds and spike-like stems across four widths and nine weights.
description: Born from a deliberate process of deleting curves and anchor points from NaN Rage Poly, Zipp reduces letterforms to their sharpest possible geometry. Round letters become diamonds balanced on a point, stems narrow to spiky slivers, and set text builds an irregular, sculptural texture. The family spans Standard, Narrow and XCondensed widths, each in nine weights from Thin to Black, covering 305 Latin-based languages and forming part of the wider NaN Rage Typographic Universe.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage Zipp as an experiment gone wrong, pushing the idea of deleting curves and points from a font to its limit. They note that round letters become diamonds sitting on their tips, stems become spiky toothpicks, and in paragraphs the typeface builds a texture full of surprises, each letter sitting almost like a small sculpture on the page. They link it to the glitch aesthetic and to German expressionist woodcuts, and position it within the broader NaN Rage Typographic Universe.

name: NaN Serf
designer: Daria Cohen, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge
classification_notes: NaN describe it as a present-day old-style serif; orthogonal terminals and open counters place it firmly in the old-style genre while the flat, squared detailing gives it a contemporary, vectorial sensibility.
summary: A contemporary old-style serif from NaN available in four optical sizes, eight weights and two punctuation variants, built to perform from micro captions to large-scale display work.
description: Orthogonal terminals and flat punctuation give this old-style serif a sharp, vectorial character that reads as warm at small sizes and crisp at display scale. Four optical cuts — Display, Headline, Text and Micro — cover the full range of typographic environments, while two stylistic versions offer squared dots and punctuation (A) or traditional rounded forms (B). Italics were added across all eight weights in a 2024 update, with monospaced variants flagged as a possible future addition.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Serf as a present-day old-style serif built to excel at all sizes, with perpendicular terminals, open counters and flat terminal-endings that behave well on screen through sharp pixel-snapping. It covers 305 Latin-based languages, comes in TTF and WOFF2 formats with autohinting, and is available in two versions: Serf A with stylised squared punctuation and Serf B with traditional rounded forms. A companion sans, NaN Serf Sans, is also available.

name: NaN Tresor
designer: Christoph Koeberlin
classification_notes: The typeface draws on historical revival material with varied structural approaches across styles, suggesting a transitional serif lineage. Its optical size range, swash caps and ornamental alternates place it firmly in the classical serif tradition with display ambitions.
summary: An anachronous serif revival by Christoph Koeberlin that deliberately preserves glyph variation across styles rather than rationalising them into a single consistent structure.
description: Rather than resolving the inconsistencies typical of historical source material, Koeberlin kept them, producing a family where structural variation between styles is a feature rather than an oversight. Three optical cuts span small-text spacing (S) through all-purpose (M) to tightly packed display settings (L), with the Swash subfamily adding ornamental caps and swashes by default. Each style carries roughly eight alternates per glyph, giving users a wide range of period textures from a single font file, supported across 420 Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Tresor as an 'anachronous revival' by Christoph Koeberlin, designed to preserve rather than rationalise the structural inconsistencies found in historical type. The family is available in three optical sizes — S for small text, M for general use and L for titles — alongside a Swash variant with ornamental caps active by default, all offering up to eight alternates per glyph.

name: NaN Rage
designer: Hugues Gentile, Fanny Hamelin, Fadhl Haqq, Léon Hugues, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Luke Prowse, Florian Runge, Jolana Sýkorová
classification_notes: A multi-family typographic system spanning slab serifs, grotesque sans-serifs, rounded, script and experimental styles, making it most accurately categorised as a display-oriented type system drawing from 19th-century wood-type and early computing references.
summary: Seven matched font families in one system, drawing on 19th-century titling wood-types and early computer fonts across slab, sans, rounded, script and experimental styles.
description: NaN Rage is a collection of seven distinct but harmonised families — Beau, Slab, Sans, Soft, Poly, Quik and Zipp — sharing proportions, contrast and language support across 305 Latin-based languages. Each family spans Extra-Condensed to Standard widths and Thin to Black in weight, offering 36 styles per subfamily. The system is positioned as ahistorical, pulling reference from Victorian wood-type titling and early bitmap computing without anchoring itself to either period.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Rage as a Typographic Universe: a collection of seven matching font families paying homage to 19th-century titling wood-types (slab serifs, early sans-serifs, rounded forms) and early computer fonts. They position it as an ahistorical type system for the present, offering a wide palette from slab and sans to script and what they call a 'weirdo', all with matching proportions, weights and language coverage. The full system is available for a starting price of 450€, structured as seven families for the price of two.

name: NaN Spaceland
designer: Jack Llewellyn
classification_notes: Built on a mono-linear grotesque skeleton but distinguished by Boolean-geometry overlapping outlines that create simultaneous positive and negative space effects; the experimental construction and illustrative qualities at larger sizes push it firmly into display territory.
summary: A grotesque with overlapping outlines derived from Boolean geometry, creating letterforms where inner and outer space become deliberately ambiguous.
description: Designed by Jack Llewellyn and released with NaN between 2022 and 2024, Spaceland applies the Boolean intersection principle to a mono-linear grotesque structure, producing outlines that fold over themselves like a Möbius strip. In lighter weights the breaks are subtle and close to elegant; in the heaviest styles the collisions become openly illustrative, inviting the reader to trace the intertwined forms. The glyph names reference characters from E. A. Abbott's Flatland, grounding the spatial conceit in a specific literary precedent.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Spaceland as Jack Llewellyn's debut release with the foundry, conceived around the Boolean geometric principle of two positive shapes creating a negative intersection. The overlapping outlines confound inner and outer space simultaneously, ranging from discreet elegance at lighter weights to an almost illustrative boldness at the heaviest. Supporting 310 Latin-based languages and available in TTF and WOFF2 formats, its character names draw from the three-dimensional world in E. A. Abbott's Flatland.

name: NaN Metrify
designer: Luke Prowse & J.B Morizot
classification_notes: NaN explicitly position Metrify within the neo-grotesque tradition, referencing Swiss International Style precedents while extending the genre across nine widths, six weights and multiple world scripts. The three ABC flavours (A, B, C) operate within the neo-grotesk skeleton with varying degrees of detail and geometric influence.
summary: A 108-style-per-variant neo-grotesque family available in three distinct flavours and seven writing systems, built as a genuinely international sans for contemporary branding.
description: Designed by Luke Prowse and J.B Morizot with specialist consultants for each script, Metrify spans nine widths from Compressed to Gigawide across six weights with matching italics, all delivered as a variable font. Three versions, A, B and C, share a common skeleton but differ in detail density: A carries industrial warmth, B strips back to a rational minimum, and C pushes toward simplified geometric forms. Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Thai and Pan-African versions extend the family beyond the Latin-centric conventions of its historical precedents.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Metrify as their 'behemoth, no-bullshit take on the Neo/Grotesk sans-serif genre', framing it as their first world-type family. Questioning what a sans should be in 2023, they worked with script consultants across Arabic, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Thai and Pan-African writing systems to produce a typeface that is 'truly international'. The three ABC flavours were conceived to give the neo-grotesque genre distinct voices rather than treating it as a uniform sub-genre.

name: NaN Success
designer: Jérémy Landes
classification_notes: NaN Success Titling draws directly from late Art Nouveau letterforms with an extreme ligature system and multi-width alternate glyphs; the family is designed first and foremost as a titling and display system, with text and sans companions subordinate to that purpose.
summary: A three-subfamily display system by Jérémy Landes built around 280 ligatures drawn from late Art Nouveau sources, spanning Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Designed by Jérémy Landes and released between 2021 and 2022, the family splits into three distinct but related sub-families: Success Titling, a ligature-dense display face with multi-width alternates that blend compressed and wide glyphs to mimic hand-lettering; Success Sans, which transplants the same organic curves onto a condensed gothic skeleton; and Success Text, a low-contrast workhorse tuned for long reading. The Titling sub-family offers Compressed, Normal and Wide optical widths, and the full family covers 317 Latin-based and 44 Cyrillic-based languages. Fifteen per cent of revenue goes to ClientEarth, with free licences available for environmental projects.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Success as an essay on titling typography, drawing from late Art Nouveau references including De Vinne and Louis Jou, with Jérémy Landes packing the Titling sub-family with 280 ligatures across Latin and Cyrillic. Multi-width ligatures mix compressed and wide glyphs to create a hand-lettering quality, while Success Sans distils the family's curves onto a gothic skeleton and Success Text offers a warm, low-contrast companion for continuous reading.

name: NaN Holo
designer: Luke Prowse
classification_notes: NaN describe it as a neo-Grotesk built on an industrial frame with biological disruptions; the rectangular counters paired with rounded curves place it squarely in the neo-grotesque tradition with an unusual biomechanical inflection.
summary: A neo-grotesque by Luke Prowse that pits rectangular counters against rounded curves to produce a typeface with a distinctly biomechanical tension, available across five widths and a monospaced companion.
description: Designed by Luke Prowse with additional work from Jean-Baptiste Morizot and Fátima Lázaro, the family runs from Compressed to a standard width, each sharing the same deliberate friction between hard-edged counters and organic curves. Wider-than-usual capitals give the text rhythm an open, unhurried quality that persists even as those idiosyncratic details flatten at smaller sizes into a steady workhorse. NaN Holo Mono extends the system into a monospaced cut suited to code and terminal display, completing a family equally at home in editorial display and UI contexts.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Holo as a replicant-inspired, fleshy neo-Grotesk built on an industrial frame with biological forms disrupting an otherwise machined flow. They frame its central tension as the interplay between rectangular counters and rounded curves, producing what they call an atemporal spirit that reads as both familiar and unfamiliar. Holo Mono is positioned as the code at the typeface's foundation, extending the system into monospaced territory.

name: NaN Hyena
designer: Reymund Schröder
classification_notes: A humanist sans-serif rooted in an extrapolation of Hildegard Korger's calligraphic work, with three stylistically distinct subfamilies distinguished by terminal treatment rather than weight alone.
summary: A humanist sans in three distinct subfamilies, each named for a time of day and distinguished by terminal treatment, drawn from the calligraphic legacy of Hildegard Korger.
description: Reymund Schröder conceived NaN Hyena as a sculptural exercise and an act of alternate typographic history, extrapolating from the work of 20th-century calligrapher Hildegard Korger. The three subfamilies, Sunrise, Noon and Sunset, are differentiated not by optical size or width but by terminal character: concave stems for the crisp morning feel of Sunrise, flat terminals for the settled assurance of Noon, and rounded endings with hairline connections in the warmer Sunset. NaN frame this as type impressionism, encouraging use of all three together across a single page or even a single word to create contrast through mood rather than conventional hierarchy.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Hyena as Reymund Schröder's crisp take on the humanist sans, initially conceived as a sculptural exercise and rooted in his practice of Typographic Uchronias, or alternate typographic history. Extrapolating from the work of calligrapher and type designer Hildegard Korger, Schröder named the three subfamilies after times of day, framing the contrast between them as analogous to Monet's studies of Rouen Cathedral under shifting light. The family supports 219 Latin-based languages and was released between 2021 and 2022 with additional production by Thomas Thiemich.

name: NaN Tragedy
designer: Jean-Baptiste Morizot
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif with an exaggerated calligraphic stress axis and classical skeleton, spanning optical sizes from text to display with a monospaced sibling; the diagonal stress and dramatic contrast place it firmly in the high-contrast serif tradition despite its unconventional detailing.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Jean-Baptiste Morizot that pairs a classical calligraphic skeleton with exaggerated geometric detailing across Text, Display and Mono subfamilies.
description: Built on a classical scribe's structure with contrast axis pushed to dramatic extremes, the family separates into a Text cut for running copy and a Display cut for theatrical headlines. A Mono sibling, co-designed with Léon Hugues and Daria Cohen, applies monospace constraints to the same modulated diagonal stress, producing a texture unusual in that genre. The italics are particularly expressive, and the family covers 219 Latin-based languages across TTF and WOFF2 formats.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Tragedy as the unexpected consequence of time travel, a chimera of two eras: the pace of a religious scribe meeting the geometrical precision of CERN's atom accelerator. Text applies optical corrections and an economical approach to its unconventional shapes for functional reading; Display amplifies the theatrics for headlines; and Mono pushes the contradictions furthest by applying technological constraints to that calligraphic modulation.

name: NaN Jaune
designer: Jérémy Landes
classification_notes: A closed-aperture grotesque with deliberately colliding glyph anatomy, positioning itself as an intentionally rule-breaking sans-serif; the foundry's own framing of inverting convention places it firmly in the grotesque tradition pushed to expressive extremes.
summary: A deliberately contrarian grotesque by Jérémy Landes that weaponises closed apertures and colliding anatomy across three subfamilies spanning display to text use.
description: Designed by Jérémy Landes with italic and Cyrillic by Vika Usmanova, NaN Jaune inverts conventional type wisdom by forcing together glyph elements that would typically be kept apart, with closed apertures throughout. Three subfamilies serve distinct roles: Maxi is a display-weight bruiser with short extenders; Midi works across contexts; and Mini trades in open counters and deep ink traps for sustained reading. Across all three, the family covers 339 Latin-based and 16 Cyrillic-based languages, with italic variants in every subfamily.
foundry_blurb: NaN describe Jaune as the embodiment of their motto — take a bad idea and do it well, or at least die trying. Where most type designers avoid colliding glyph anatomy and closed apertures, Jaune sticks together that which normally shouldn't touch. The family runs from Maxi (display, with a range from Thin to Black) through Midi (an all-terrain middle child) to Mini (simplified structure with open counters and ink traps for small sizes), with the full collection spanning 339 Latin-based and 16 Cyrillic-based languages.
